cataloging_subject.label: Three Middle-English Versions of the Rule of St. Benet and Two Contemporary Rituals for the Ordination of Nuns
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Ernst A. Kock's 1902 edited selection of five distinct anonymous Middle English texts: a northern prose adaptation of Benedict's Rule, a northern metrical adaptation, the Caxton-press compendious abstract, and two distinct ordination rituals respectively appended to the Lansdowne and Cotton Rule witnesses. The three Rule texts are independent version-level works derived from the same Latin Rule, not three witnesses of one Middle English work. The analogous rituals have different texts and witnesses and are not asserted to be recensions of one work. The encoded unit also contains Kock's title page and notes, but omits his introduction and glossaries.

catalog_summary.title: Three Middle-English Versions of the Rule of St. Benet and Two Contemporary Rituals for the Ordination of Nuns
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English adapters, translators, and ritual authors; Benedict of Nursia is the source author for the three Rule texts
catalog_summary.date: Rule and ritual witnesses dated to the first quarter or broader fifteenth century; Caxton abstract printed about 1491; edited selection first published 1902
catalog_summary.region: England; the Lansdowne texts are northern, Kock groups the Cotton Rule and ritual in his Northern-text linguistic discussion while noting a more southerly stamp in the metrical Rule, the British Library gives English origin for the Cotton component, and the Caxton abstract was printed at Westminster
genres: Ritual for the ordination or profession of nuns

identifiers.scheme: Library of Congress call number in source XML

Northern Prose Version of the Rule of St. Benet

Son, Herkyn þe commandementis of þe mastir
All þat wyll lely lyue in land
Here felowyth a compendious abstracte translate into englysshe out of the holy rule of saynte Benet for men and wymmen
This is þe furme how A Nouice sall be made & resaiuid to religiun
Here bygyns þe manere forto make a nun

agents.name: Benedict of Nursia
alternate_names: Saint Benedict, Abbot of Monte Cassino
notes: Source author of the Latin Rule represented through three anonymous Middle English adaptations; not the author of their Middle English wording or of either ordination ritual.

agents.name: Anonymous northern prose adapter and translator
notes: Kock demonstrates substantial paraphrase, omission, addition, and reordering; the Lansdowne copy is a transcript, not an identified authorial original.

agents.name: Anonymous northern metrical adapter and translator
Anonymous Caxton-press abstractor and translator
notes: The text asks readers to pray for its translator but supplies no name. No evidence reviewed identifies William Caxton as translator or abstractor.

agents.name: Anonymous author of the Lansdowne ordination ritual
Anonymous author of the Vespasian ordination ritual
Ernst A. Kock
notes: Editor, transcriber, collator, and commentator of the 1902 source edition; not a medieval author.

agents.name: Frederick J. Furnivall
notes: Kock credits Furnivall with the content labels in running heads on printed pp. 1-140.

agents.name: William Caxton
notes: Printer at Westminster of the approximately 1491 Book of Divers Ghostly Matters. Printer is not an available responsibility role in this schema, so his transmission-layer role is modeled in the lineage manifest and is not converted into authorship or translation.

scope.description: Authorship of the underlying Latin Rule represented through the three Middle English adaptations

notes: This source-author role excludes the anonymous Middle English wording and adaptation, both ordination rituals, and Kock's editorial interventions.

assertion.notes: Translation is mediated by free paraphrase and adaptation rather than word-for-word rendering.
The explicit requests prayer for the translator but does not name Caxton or another individual.
For previously printed texts Kock also re-edited and collated earlier transcriptions against the source witnesses.

scope.description: Modern content labels in the running heads on Kock pp. 1-140

assertion.notes: This dates the surviving witness and first known attestation, not the lost originals or adaptation acts.

date_statements.display: Cotton MS Vespasian A XXV, ff. 66-126: first quarter of the fifteenth century, with later additions

assertion.notes: The later heading to the ordination ritual is not used to redetermine the date of its main text.
The label is linguistic and editorial, not proof of one composition place or common author.

Kock treats the text in his Northern-text linguistic apparatus but says that its language has a more southerly stamp; the British Library Cotton record gives English origin without a northern-dialect claim.
This is Kock's editorial grouping of the ritual with the Northern texts, not a British Library catalog assertion; the current Cotton record gives English origin only.
Westminster is the place of printing, not a demonstrated composition or translation place.

scope.description: Chapter lemmata, liturgical formulas, prayers, and rubrics embedded in the Middle English texts

form_statements.description: Four prose works, one verse work, and modern prose notes

genre_statements.label: Ritual for the ordination or profession of nuns

summaries.value: Kock's selection juxtaposes three independently adapted Middle English forms of Benedict's Latin Rule: an extensive northern prose paraphrase for female religious, a 2,516-line verse version treated in Kock's Northern-text apparatus but described as having a more southerly stamp, and a short Caxton-press abstract addressed to religious men and women. It then prints two distinct procedures for receiving, professing, or ordaining nuns, one transmitted with the Lansdowne prose Rule and one with the Cotton metrical Rule. Kock's notes explain readings and editorial decisions.

The three Rule texts share Benedict's Latin Rule as an abstract source but differ in prose or verse form, chapter coverage, order, expansion, omission, and witness history. They are modeled as distinct Middle English works. Kock calls the Vespasian ritual an analogous appendage to the Lansdowne ritual; absent evidence of direct copying or a shared textual exemplar, the rituals are also distinct works rather than recensions.

extent.description: Five historical texts in six body DIV1 elements because Kock's notes form the sixth division. The XML has 2,516 L elements in 50 Q1 groups for the metrical Rule, 176 P elements across the five historical texts, 7 P elements in the notes, 283 NOTE1 editorial annotations, 158 PB markers, and 247 manuscript or signature MILESTONE elements. The encoded title page precedes the body. Counts describe the encoding, not an inferred original medieval structure.

content_parts.label: Encoded title page
content_parts.summary: Kock's edition title, editorship statement, and 1902 imprint.

content_parts.label: I. Northern Prose Version of the Rule of St. Benet
content_parts.summary: An anonymous extensive prose translation and adaptation for female religious, surviving in Lansdowne MS 378.

content_parts.label: II. Northern Metrical Version of the Rule of St. Benet
content_parts.summary: An anonymous 2,516-line verse translation and adaptation for women, surviving in Cotton MS Vespasian A XXV.

locators.value: DIV1 NODE AHA2736:2; PB 48-118; 2,516 L elements

content_parts.label: III. Caxton Abstract of the Rule of St. Benet
content_parts.summary: An anonymous compendious prose abstract of Benedict's Rule printed as the third part of Caxton's Book of Divers Ghostly Matters, for religious men and women with little or no Latin.

content_parts.label: IV. Northern Lansdowne Ritual for the Ordination of Nuns
content_parts.summary: An anonymous prose procedure for receiving a novice and making her profession, transmitted immediately after the prose Rule in Lansdowne MS 378.

content_parts.label: V. Vespasian Ritual for the Ordination of Nuns
content_parts.summary: An anonymous prose and Latin-formula procedure for making a nun, transmitted after the metrical Rule in Cotton MS Vespasian A XXV and previously printed by Maskell.

content_parts.label: Notes
content_parts.summary: Kock's textual, linguistic, and explanatory notes on the first four edited texts; the section has no separate Vespasian-ritual division.

locators.value: DIV1 NODE AHA2736:6; PB 151-157, continuing through printed p. 158

related_works.title: Rule of St Benedict
notes: Shared abstract Latin source for the three Rule parts only; it is not the authorial source for the two ordination rituals.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source XML aha2736, pinned CME submodule artifact.
evidence.locator: IDG AHA2736; title page; BODY DIV1 nodes AHA2736:1-AHA2736:6
evidence.summary: Supplies the exact encoded title page, five historical texts, editorial notes, printed-page markers, manuscript-folio milestones, language, and editorial annotations.

evidence.locator: Complete element inventory and per-DIV1 counts
evidence.summary: Verified six BODY DIV1 elements; 138 DIV2; 29 DIV3; 2,516 L; 50 Q1; 186 P overall; 176 P in the five historical texts; 283 NOTE1; 158 PB; 247 MILESTONE; and the exact printed-page and folio ranges used in the content parts.

evidence.citation: Ernst A. Kock, ed., Three Middle-English Versions of the Rule of St. Benet and Two Contemporary Rituals for the Ordination of Nuns (London: Early English Text Society, 1902).
evidence.locator: title page; contents; introduction pp. ix-xlix; texts pp. 1-150; notes pp. 151-158
evidence.summary: The complete edition establishes Kock's selection boundary, the five separate texts, source witnesses, prior editions, manuscript descriptions, editorial method, anonymous adaptation and translation, Caxton's printing role, and the distinct but analogous status of the two rituals.

evidence.citation: British Library, Archives and Manuscripts catalogue, Lansdowne MS 378, record 040-002074831.
evidence.summary: Describes a fifteenth-century vellum manuscript in a northern dialect containing an English exposition of Benedict's Rule followed by a form for making or receiving a novice.

evidence.citation: British Library, Archives and Manuscripts catalogue, Cotton MS Vespasian A XXV, ff. 66-126, record 041-001103216.
evidence.summary: Identifies the Rule made for use in a nunnery and Here bygyns the manere forto make a nun, their current folio ranges, Middle English and Latin languages, first-quarter-fifteenth-century date with later additions, English origin, and possible former owners Henry Savile and John Anstis.

evidence.citation: Karl Böddeker, Versifizirte Benediktinerregel im northern dialect, Englische Studien 2 (1879), 60-93.
evidence.summary: First prints the complete Cotton metrical Rule from Cotton Vespasian A XXV, dates its copying to the early fifteenth century, recognizes it as a copy rather than an original manuscript, and supplies the prior text re-edited by Kock.

evidence.citation: Ferdinand Holthausen, Die nördliche Benedictinerregel, Anglia 14 (1892), 302-308.
evidence.summary: Offers emendations and linguistic commentary on Böddeker's printed metrical text. It is supporting criticism, not an independent target transcription or Kock's physical witness.

evidence.citation: Eugen Kölbing, manuscript collation of the northern Benedictine Rule, in Beiträge zur Textkritik und Erklärung der Liederdichtung des 16. Jahrhunderts, Englische Studien 23 (1897), 284-286.
evidence.summary: Reports a fresh comparison of Böddeker's edition against Cotton Vespasian A XXV and lists corrections while considering Holthausen's emendations; Kock cites this collation.

evidence.citation: William Maskell, The Manner to Make a Nun, Monumenta Ritualia Ecclesiae Anglicanae, 2nd ed., vol. 3 (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1882), 360-363.
evidence.summary: Prints the complete Vespasian ordination ritual from the Cotton manuscript before Kock; Kock re-edits the same ritual with manuscript collation.

evidence.citation: CERL Incunabula Short Title Catalogue, ig00301000, Book of Divers Ghostly Matters.
evidence.summary: Identifies the three-part Westminster printing by William Caxton about 1491, includes the Rule of St Benedict, lists the Cambridge University Library holdings, and supplies standard bibliographic identifiers.

evidence.citation: Morgan Library & Museum, PML 704, The Book of Divers Ghostly Matters.
evidence.summary: Confirms Caxton's Westminster imprint about 1491, the three-part collation, and the Rule's 20-leaf aa-bb8 cc4 extent. The Morgan copy lacks multiple Rule leaves and is supporting collateral, not Kock's Cambridge source copy.

evidence.citation: Early English Books Online Text Creation Partnership, B11684, Book of Divers Ghostly Matters, TEI P5 XML.
evidence.locator: third grouped text; TCP image references 159995:130-149
evidence.summary: Provides a complete collateral transcription of the Caxton abstract within a composite transcription made from several microfilm copies, gives STC 3305 and ESTC S1821, identifies the anonymous abstract as derived from Benedict's Rule, and applies CC0 1.0 to the TCP encoding.

open_questions.question: Which Latin or intermediary exemplars underlie each of the three Middle English Rule adaptations?
next_steps: Compare each adaptation against modern critical recensions of Benedict's Latin Rule and other vernacular Rule traditions.
Do not infer one shared Middle English ancestor merely from the shared Latin source.

open_questions.question: Do the Lansdowne and Vespasian ordination rituals share a recoverable textual ancestor beyond analogous institutional function?
next_steps: Collate the full rituals against medieval English profession and consecration ordines.
Retain separate-work modeling unless textual dependence or a recension relationship is demonstrated.

open_questions.question: Can any adapter, translator, ritual author, or manuscript scribe be identified more precisely?
next_steps: Consult manuscript hand descriptions, institutional provenance research, and current specialist bibliography.
Keep Caxton as printer rather than translator or abstractor absent positive evidence.

notes: The XML encodes the edition title page and printed pp. 1-158. Kock's introduction (pp. ix-xlix) and glossaries (pp. 159-218) remain outside the U-M/General Corpus text.
Benedict is the author of the abstract Latin Rule source, not the direct author of the three differently adapted Middle English works or the rituals.
Caxton is securely the printer of the approximately 1491 source book. The reviewed sources do not identify him as translator, abstractor, or author of the Rule text.

Witness dates are first-attestation evidence, not silently converted into composition dates. Unknown scribes remain unknown; the Cotton catalog's Henry Savile and John Anstis are possible former owners, not textual creators.
